lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Sialylation and anti-inflammatory IgG
Now we come to what is, in some ways, the most counterintuitive part of the Fc glycan story.
Because everything we've talked about so far has been about making antibodies more active — boosting ADCC, boosting CDC.
But the same Asn297 glycan, with a different sugar at its tip, can make antibodies less active.
Specifically, anti-inflammatory.
And that fact underlies one of the biggest unsolved mysteries — and biggest commercial markets — in clinical immunology: the mechanism of intravenous immunoglobulin, IVIG.
IVIG is a therapy in which polyclonal IgG is purified from the pooled plasma of thousands of healthy donors and infused into patients with autoimmune or inflammatory diseases.
It's used in idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, in Guillain-Barré syndrome, in Kawasaki disease, in chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, in a long list of indications.
It works.
Patients improve.
Inflammation subsides.
And for a long time, nobody knew exactly why a bag of pooled normal IgG would suppress inflammation, when individual IgG molecules are typically thought of as inflammatory.

The answer, worked out largely by Jeffrey Ravetch and his collaborators at Rockefeller University in the 2000s and 2010s, is that a small fraction of the IgG in IVIG — perhaps 5% — carries a particular Fc glycoform, namely a sialylated, galactosylated, biantennary structure.
Sialic acid on the terminal end of the glycan, cap on the galactose, full biantennary.
And this sialylated subset, despite being a minority of the IVIG preparation, is responsible for most of the anti-inflammatory activity.

The mechanism Ravetch proposed — and it's still being refined — involves the sialylated Fc binding to a different receptor than ordinary IgG: not FcγRIIIa, but a C-type lectin called DC-SIGN on dendritic cells and certain macrophages, or in mouse models a related receptor called SIGN-R1.

Binding of sialylated Fc to these lectin receptors triggers a signaling cascade that ultimately upregulates the inhibitory Fc receptor FcγRIIB on effector cells, raising the threshold for those cells to be activated by other immune complexes.
Net effect: the cell becomes less responsive to inflammatory signals.
Inflammation goes down.
If this is right — and there is robust evidence for it, though the mechanism remains debated in some details — then sialylated IgG is, functionally, an anti-inflammatory cytokine.
The same molecule type that, when fucosylated and non-sialylated, drives ADCC and inflammation, when sialylated drives the opposite.
This is the inverse of the afucose story, and the analogy I want you to hold is: the sialylated Fc is anti-inflammatory; this is part of how IVIG works in autoimmune disease.
Same Asn297 glycan.
Different tip-sugar.
Opposite immune signal.
The therapeutic implication is large.

If IVIG works because of its sialylated fraction, then maybe you don't need to give patients liters of plasma-pooled IgG — you could give them a recombinant, fully sialylated Fc preparation, at a fraction of the dose, with a defined product.
Several companies have pursued this.
Recombinant sialylated Fcs and sialylated IgGs have been engineered, and some are in clinical development as next-generation IVIG substitutes for autoimmune indications.

The challenge has been getting sialylation high enough — most CHO cells make IgG with only a few percent sialylation, so you need either cell-line engineering, enzymatic post-modification, or specialized glycoengineering approaches to drive sialylation to therapeutic levels.
This is an active area, and I won't predict which approach wins.
But I will tell you the conceptual point: glycoengineering is not just about boosting potency.
It is also about programming the immune signal in either direction.

The same molecular chassis — the IgG Fc — can be tuned to be more inflammatory, less inflammatory, more cytotoxic, less cytotoxic, more complement-fixing, less complement-fixing, all by choosing different decorations on the Asn297 glycan.
That tunability is the deeper promise of the field.
